Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic because I don't have internet at home, so I had to plan everything around when I could get to campus or find a café with wifi. I'd usually try to batch my online work—downloading everything I could when I had access, then studying from those materials later. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often rewrite notes by hand or create physical flashcards from what I downloaded. My extracurricular activities also meant I had to squeeze study time into weird pockets throughout the week.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on when I could actually get online and what deadlines were coming up. Some weeks I'd have more regular access if I stayed late on campus after my activities, but other weeks everything got compressed into one or two intense sessions. I tried to front-load downloading materials early in the week so I could study offline later. Deadlines definitely pushed me to find internet access no matter what, even if it meant going out of my way.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was something specific I needed to download or check—like new assignment instructions or lecture materials. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if anything had been posted that I'd need for the coming days. The discussion posts were another reason, since I tried to participate when I could, but that required being online in the moment. Honestly, a lot of my logins were strategic rather than casual browsing.
Q4: It never really became a daily routine because of my internet situation, but I did develop a pattern of logging in during my campus time between activities. I'd treat it like a checklist—download videos, check announcements, post in discussions if I had something ready. After a few weeks, I automatically knew to head to the library before my extracurricular meetings to get my LMS tasks done. It was more of a scheduled ritual than a natural habit.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful because I could download them and watch offline multiple times. I'm kinesthetic, so I'd often pause and take physical notes or even act out processes to understand them better. The discussion posts were also valuable because they helped me see how others understood the material, which filled gaps in my own thinking. Anything I could save and work with hands-on later was gold for me.
Q6: I tended to skip or delay the supplementary readings that were only available online without download options. If I couldn't save it for later, it often got pushed aside because my online time was limited. Some of the interactive quizzes I also used minimally because they required constant internet connection, which I just couldn't maintain. I knew they might help, but I had to prioritize what I could actually access reliably.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to assignments or exams, or if I could engage with it physically somehow. If I could download it, print it, or turn it into something tangible, it felt valuable. I also appreciated activities that gave me immediate feedback because with my limited online time, I needed to know quickly if I understood something or not. Time is precious when you're working around access issues.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I often couldn't afford. With required activities, I'd make sure to get them done no matter what—I'd find internet access somehow. But optional ones? They usually got cut when I was pressed for time or access. It's not that I didn't see their value; I just had to triage constantly. Sometimes I'd save optional materials to look at during breaks, but that was rare.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download lecture videos and PDFs, then study from them at home without any internet. I also borrowed textbooks from the library and took extensive handwritten notes. Sometimes I'd meet with peers from my extracurricular groups to study together in person—we'd quiz each other and work through problems on whiteboards. A huge portion of my learning happened completely off the LMS radar.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be at home reviewing downloaded materials, rewriting notes, or practicing problems from printed worksheets. None of that showed up anywhere online. I'd also spend time during commutes reviewing flashcards I'd made by hand. Sometimes I'd have long study sessions with friends where we'd work through concepts together, but since we weren't online, the LMS had no idea. My engagement was real, just invisible.
Q11: All the time—that was basically my strategy. I'd access a video or document once, download it immediately, and then never open it on the LMS again. I'd watch lectures multiple times offline, pausing to take notes and really absorb the material. The LMS would show I accessed something once, but I might have spent hours with that same material offline. It was the only way I could make things work without reliable internet.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when assignments piled up and my extracurricular commitments intensified. Without internet at home, catching up felt overwhelming because I couldn't just hop online whenever. Stress would build up, and even though my anxiety stayed pretty manageable, I'd feel this weight of falling behind. Those were the times my attendance dropped because I was trying to juggle too many things at once.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from activities or had competing responsibilities, my LMS usage dropped significantly. I'd prioritize downloading only the most essential materials and skip the rest. Confusion was tough too—if I didn't understand something, I couldn't just Google it at home, so I'd have to wait until I had access again. Fatigue made me less efficient during my limited online windows, which was frustrating.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd log in and just go through the motions—clicking through materials without really absorbing anything because I was tired or distracted. There were times I'd have a video playing while my mind was elsewhere, worrying about other responsibilities. I'd technically be 'active' on the LMS, but I wasn't actually learning. Those sessions felt wasted, and I'd have to revisit the material later offline to actually understand it.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts more than I should have because they required being online and thinking on my feet, which was hard with limited access. I also avoided some of the longer readings when I was stressed, even knowing they'd help. It wasn't laziness—it was more like survival mode, where I had to choose between what was urgent and what was important. Sometimes the important things lost out to immediate pressures.
Q16: Deadlines completely shaped my LMS usage. I'd plan my internet access around when things were due, making sure I could submit on time. The days before deadlines, I'd be much more active online—downloading last-minute materials, posting in discussions, checking requirements. Without deadlines, I might go longer stretches without logging in because I was studying offline. Deadlines were like anchors that pulled me back to the platform.
Q17: Feedback motivated me a lot, especially when I did well despite all my challenges. Seeing that I scored 98.75 on an assignment after all that offline grinding felt validating. When feedback was quick, I could adjust my approach before the next deadline. But when it was delayed, I'd sometimes move on without knowing if I was on the right track, which was stressful. Good feedback made me want to engage more.
Q18: The course structure helped in some ways—having clear modules meant I could download everything for a section at once. But the pacing sometimes assumed constant online access, which didn't match my reality. When things were spread out with regular deadlines, I could plan better. When multiple things clustered together, I struggled to get enough online time to keep up with everything simultaneously.
Q19: Downloadable materials made engagement way easier for me—anything I could save and access offline was a lifesaver. The discussion requirements were harder because they demanded real-time online presence. I appreciated when instructors posted materials early so I could download ahead of time. What made things harder was when content was only viewable online or required constant connectivity for interactive elements.
Q20: I think students might be exploring, trying to understand the system, or looking for resources without knowing exactly what they need. Some might be dealing with issues like mine—downloading things to use later, so their activity looks scattered. Others might be struggling to understand the material, so they keep revisiting without producing output yet. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity; sometimes it's searching for clarity.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My LMS activity was relatively low because I did so much offline, but I still earned a Merit grade and did well on assignments. When you're strategic about what you access and then put in serious work offline, you can perform well without high platform engagement. The LMS only sees one slice of the effort. Students like me might look disengaged when we're actually working hard elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when students log in, what they click on, submission times. That's useful for identifying who might be struggling with access or timing. But logs completely miss offline studying, physical note-taking, group study sessions, and the quality of engagement during online time. They can't tell if someone watched a video attentively or had it playing in the background. Quantity of clicks isn't quality of learning.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively wrestling with the material, trying to understand it deeply enough to apply it. For me, that often looks like rewriting notes by hand, discussing concepts with peers, or working through practice problems until they click. It's not about how many times I log in—it's about whether I'm mentally present and putting in genuine effort to learn. Engagement is internal work that doesn't always leave digital traces.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied from downloaded materials at home, or the hours I spent making handwritten notes and flashcards. It wouldn't capture my study sessions with friends from extracurriculars, or how I'd review concepts during commutes. It also wouldn't show my determination to succeed despite not having internet at home—that resilience and problem-solving to work around my limitations. My real engagement story is mostly invisible to the data.
